subject.title: Physics
subject.description:
**IB Physics** is a **Group 4** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **space, time and motion; the particulate nature of matter; wave behaviour; fields; and nuclear and quantum physics**, with a strong focus on **experimental work, data analysis, and scientific investigation skills**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2025**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Multiple-choice questions and data-based questions | 1 hour 30 minutes | 1 hour 30 minutes | 30% |
| **Paper 2** | Short-answer and extended-response questions | 1 hour 30 minutes | 2 hours 15 minutes | 50% |
| **Internal Assessment** | Individual scientific investigation | 10 hours | 10 hours | 20% |

strengths: Data tables include clear headings, units and instrumental uncertainties.
Processed results are displayed correctly and a graph with error bars is supplied.
An example calculation demonstrates correct processing procedures.
weaknesses: Axes on the main graph are mislabeled, reducing precision of communication.
Uncertainty propagation is not shown and the Â±0.3 cm videoâmeasurement error is asserted rather than quantitatively justified.
Raw and processed data are merged in tables without consistent units or clear separation, affecting clarity.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Communication of data is mostly clear and reasonably precise (band 4), but consideration of uncertainties and dataâprocessing quality contain significant omissions (bands 3). Overall performance sits in the 3â4 range and justifies a score of 4.

strengths: The research question is situated in a detailed, specific bungeeâjumping context with clear realâworld relevance.
Apparatus, dimensions, masses and trial procedures are described sufficiently to allow reproduction with few ambiguities.
Controlled variables are justified and held constant, with consideration of sufficiency of the independentâvariable range.
weaknesses: Quantitative background context (e.g. typical jumper masses, commercial cord parameters) is omitted, limiting depth of realâworld framing.
Camera placement, frameârate and videoâanalysis procedures are not fully specified, introducing minor ambiguities.
The method for estimating and reporting measurement uncertainties (Â±0.1 cm) is not explained, hindering complete reproducibility.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Two of three strands meet the top band (5â6) criteria (context specificity and methodological considerations explained), while one strand (dataâcollection protocol) has minor omissions. On balance, the work clearly allows reproduction with few ambiguities and situates the question in a specific context, meriting a holistic score of 5.

strengths: The conclusion correctly identifies that extension increases with mass and ties this to the plotted trend.
Anomalies are acknowledged and linked qualitatively to energyâloss mechanisms.
The discussion references the energyâconversion equation, providing relevant comparison to accepted scientific theory.
weaknesses: No quantitative extraction of a spring constant from the slope is provided to fully justify the conclusion.
The role of non-ideal factors (heat loss, air resistance) is mentioned but not quantitatively linked to the observed anomalies.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The conclusion is well justified and consistent with the data, and it engages with scientific context beyond a superficial level. While deeper quantitative analysis is lacking, the qualitative comparison and anomaly discussion elevate it into the 5â6 band, warranting a score of 5.

strengths: Specific methodological weaknesses (camera blur, ruler calibration, dropâheight ceiling) are identified with discussion of their impact on results.
Realistic improvements (higher frameârate camera, closer ruler placement, instrument calibration) are proposed.
Explanations link how each suggested improvement would reduce uncertainty and enhance data quality.
weaknesses: Quantitative estimates of how each improvement reduces error (e.g. percentage uncertainty reduction) are missing.
Suggestions lack prioritization or discussion of feasibility and resource implications.
Calibration procedures for measuring instruments are recommended but not detailed.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The evaluation describes specific limitations and explains their relative impact on accuracy, and it offers realistic, relevant improvements with rationale. Though more quantitative depth would strengthen it, the work meets the 5â6 band descriptors and merits a score of 5.

strandSelections.reasoning: The student clearly situates the investigation within the real-world context of bungee jumping and repeatedly links the research question to energy transformations in an elastic cord.  Specific parameters of the scenario (fixed drop height, elastic string behaviour) are described, so the reader understands exactly why this question matters and how it will be explored.  Although the context could be deepened with quantitative references to commercial bungee operations, the description is unquestionably specific and appropriate rather than merely broad.

Instrument uncertainties are quoted (Â±0.01 g, Â±0.3 cm) and shown on the graph as error bars, but uncertainty propagation through calculations is absent and the choice of Â±0.3 cm for a video-based ruler reading is asserted rather than justified quantitatively.  Thus, consideration is present but contains significant omissions.

The student explains why each controlled variable (drop height, initial string length) must remain constant and details how it was held constant.  The independent-variable range is justified (30â150 g) with reference to readability and string failure, demonstrating consideration of sufficiency.  Methodological limitations (camera blur, elastic limit) are discussed and linked to data quality, showing an explicit explanation of considerations relevant to collecting data adequate to answer the question.

Specific weaknesses (reading error, camera placement, drop-height ceiling) are identified and their significance for accuracy or number of measurements is described.  The impact on final results is mentioned qualitatively, but relative magnitude (e.g., % error) is not analysed, so the explanation is competent rather than thorough.

Apparatus, dimensions, masses, number of trials, camera use and step-by-step instructions are all provided, so another student could largely reproduce the work.  However, several ambiguities remain: the exact position/angle of the camera, how the frame-by-frame analysis was carried out, and how the mid-air ruler reading was converted into centimetres are not fully specified.  These minor gaps keep the description at the level of âfew ambiguitiesâ rather than completely unambiguous.

Averages and extensions are calculated correctly and an example calculation is shown.  However, several raw-to-processed entries are internally inconsistent, and no statistical treatment (e.g., standard deviation) accompanies the averages.  Hence processing is generally appropriate but marred by some inaccuracies and omissions.

For each listed limitation the student provides a realistic improvement (closer ruler, higher-frame-rate camera, higher drop point).  The suggestions are relevant but the explanations of HOW they would reduce uncertainty are brief, lacking quantitative justification or feasibility discussion, fitting the descriptor of âdescribedâ rather than âexplainedâ.

Data tables include headings, units and stated instrumental uncertainties; the processed results are displayed and a graph with error bars is supplied.  Minor formatting issues (misaligned columns, two merged tables) and occasional missing units reduce precision, but overall the communication is both readable and mostly precise.

Reference is made to the energy-conversion equation and the concept of an elastic limit, providing some comparison to accepted theory.  The comparison remains qualitative and does not, for example, extract a spring constant or discuss Hookeâs law limits, so the alignment with scientific context is relevant but not fully developed.

The student states that extension increases with mass and supports this with the plotted trend and discussion of two anomalies.  The conclusion is largely consistent with the data but acknowledges an unexpected decrease in gradient without fully analysing it quantitatively, preventing a fully justified rating.

annotations.comment: The student situates the research question clearly but could enhance specificity by including quantitative context such as typical jumper masses and bungee cord parameters to deepen real-world relevance.

Recommendation to increase frame rate and use a more versatile tripod is realistic but lacks explanation of how these changes quantitatively reduce error. Detail expected improvements (e.g.	motion blur reduction metrics).
The limitations table on drop height suggests shorter string or higher drop, but priorities and feasibility are unclear. Order suggestions by impact and discuss safety implications for each.
Reflection on time efficiency is self-aware but omits consideration of trade-off between speed and data precision. Discuss whether faster data collection compromised measurement accuracy.

Background theory invokes the ideal energy conservation equation $$mg\Delta h=\tfrac{1}{2}kX^2$$ without acknowledging non-ideal factors. The student should discuss assumptions and potential energy losses to justify the model.

The hypothesis restates energy theory but could be strengthened by predicting the functional relationship (e.g.
linear gradient proportional to $k$) and specifying how graph parameters will confirm or refute the claim.

Controlled-variable descriptions include values and uncertainties but omit details of how uncertainties (Â±0.1 cm) were determined. Clarify measurement procedures and uncertainty estimation to enable reproducibility without ambiguity.

Justification of mass range (30â150 g) is descriptive but lacks quantitative rationale (e.g. orce or stress limits). Include calculations showing why these limits ensure data sufficiency and string integrity.

The experimental diagram is clear, but critical dimensions (e.g.
camera distance and angle) are not labelled. Add these annotations to allow precise replication of the setup.
Procedure is thorough but lacks specification of camera placement (height, orientation) and video analysis software or frame rate. Include these details to eliminate ambiguities in data collection.
The stated error of Â±0.3 cm for video measurements is asserted but not justified with pixel-to-cm resolution or frame-exposure considerations. Provide a quantitative basis for this uncertainty.
Although the photograph illustrates maximum extension, the methodology would benefit from a calibration frame (e.g.uler within frame) to verify scale and reduce parallax error.
Repeating each drop three times reduces random error, but the student could calculate standard deviation of the three trials to quantify uncertainty instead of only averaging.
Precautions address safety and waste but omit data-quality controls (e.g.uler calibration before each trial). Include these measures to demonstrate comprehensive methodological consideration.
The raw data table merges raw and processed columns, reducing clarity and precision. Separate raw measurements and processed calculations into distinct, well-labelled tables with consistent units.
Headings in both raw and processed tables sometimes omit units and uncertainties. Ensure each column label clearly states units (cm, g) and associated uncertainties for precise communication.
Justification of reading errors is qualitative; the student should quantify how camera blur contributes to the stated Â±0.3 cm uncertainty using frame rate, pixel size or image analysis.
Example calculation demonstrates correct arithmetic but does not state rounding conventions. Specify how values are rounded to maintain consistency in significant figures.
Axes in Graph 1 are mislabeled: extension should be on the y-axis and mass on the x-axis. Correct axis labels and ensure units match data for clear communication.
The graph omits a trendline equation and goodness-of-fit metric (e.g. RÂ²). Including these would quantify the linear relationship and strengthen data interpretation.

Conclusion correctly states that extension increases with mass but lacks quantitative support. The student should calculate the spring constant $$k$$ from the slope to justify consistency with theory.
The conclusion discusses neglecting energy losses but does not link these to observed anomalies. Discuss how heat or air resistance could produce the two deviant data points.
Strengths of controls are well described, but the evaluation omits calibration of measuring instruments. Include device calibration to further demonstrate methodological rigor.
Identified limitations are specific, but the impact statements lack quantitative context. Estimate percentage error from reading inaccuracies to clarify their relative significance.

rubricData.rubricSpecificInfo: âThe Physics Internal Assessment (IA) is a critical component of the IB Diploma Programme, providing students with the opportunity to design and conduct independent investigations into physics phenomena. This assessment emphasizes the development of practical skills, analytical thinking, and a deep understanding of scientific methodologies. By engaging in the IA, students not only apply theoretical knowledge to real-world scenarios but also cultivate competencies in research design, data analysis, and critical evaluation, all of which are essential for academic and professional success in scientific fields. â
rubricData.name: Physics IA
